metadata.summary: Integrate reranking into RAG pipelines: RerankingRAG class, batch processing, fallback handling, agent workflow, and production tool/service selection.

content.title: Reranking Pipeline Integration for RAG
content.summary: Integrate reranking into a production RAG pipeline by wrapping the two-stage retrieve + rerank flow in a RerankingRAG class, supporting cross-encoder, Cohere API, and LLM-based rerankers. Includes batch reranking with ThreadPoolExecutor, production fallback pattern, and agentic workflow guidance.

why.p: A bare cross-encoder call is sufficient for experiments but production RAG needs: configurable reranker backends (local vs managed API), graceful fallback when the reranker is unavailable, batch processing for high-throughput scenarios, and caching to avoid repeated scoring of identical query/document pairs. Without a structured integration layer, reranking becomes a fragile ad-hoc step that breaks pipelines when the reranker times out or rate-limits.

list.item: Building a production RAG pipeline that needs to switch between local cross-encoder and Cohere/Voyage API rerankers.
High-throughput systems processing multiple queries simultaneously that need parallel batch reranking.
Any pipeline where the reranker is on the critical path and must degrade gracefully to initial retrieval order on failure.
Agentic systems where a retrieval subagent passes candidates to a downstream generation agent.
Single-query scripts or notebooks — the full integration class adds overhead without value.
When the reranker is always local and always available — skip the fallback machinery.
Cost is the primary constraint and Cohere/API reranking fees are prohibitive at scale — use a local cross-encoder only.

section.name: reranking-rag-class
section.p: The RerankingRAG class wraps vector store retrieval and reranking into a single .retrieve() interface. The caller sets initial_top_k (candidate pool size), rerank_top_k (final returned results), and reranker backend (cross-encoder, cohere, or llm). This makes the reranker swappable without changing downstream generation logic.

example.title: RerankingRAG: configurable two-stage retrieval class
example.note: Switch reranker backend by changing config.reranker — cross-encoder for local, cohere for managed API, llm for highest quality at highest cost.

rule.statement: When using the Cohere Rerank API, always map results back to original documents using r.index, never by list position. Cohere returns results in score order, not input order.
rule.rationale: Cohere's response.results is sorted by relevance score descending. Using list position instead of r.index maps rerank scores to the wrong documents, silently corrupting the ranking.

section.name: batch-reranking
section.p: For high-throughput pipelines processing multiple queries simultaneously, batch reranking with ThreadPoolExecutor parallelizes the reranking step across queries. Each worker processes one (query, documents) pair independently.

example.title: Batch reranking with parallel workers
example.code:
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor from typing import List, Tuple import numpy as np
def batch_rerank( queries: List[str], document_lists: List[List[str]], reranker, top_k: int = 5, max_workers: int = 4 ) -> List[List[dict]]: """ Rerank multiple query-documents pairs in parallel. """ def rerank_single(args: Tuple[str, List[str]]) -> List[dict]: query, documents = args pairs = [[query, doc] for doc in documents] scores = reranker.predict(pairs)
sorted_indices = np.argsort(scores)[::-1][:top_k] return [ {"document": documents[i], "score": float(scores[i])} for i in sorted_indices ]
with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=max_workers) as executor: results = list(executor.map( rerank_single, zip(queries, document_lists) ))
return results
example.note: max_workers=4 is a safe starting point. Test with realistic candidate pool sizes; large pools times many workers can hit ulimit on open file descriptors.

antipattern.description: Using batch reranking with ThreadPoolExecutor without testing realistic candidate pool sizes and worker counts on the target infrastructure.
antipattern.reason: Large candidate_pool_size x max_workers can exhaust ulimit on open file descriptors. Test under load before deploying.

section.name: fallback-pattern
section.p: In production, the reranker is on the critical path. If it times out, throws an exception, or becomes unavailable, the query must still return results. The fallback is to return the initial retrieval order, which is better than failing the query entirely.

example.title: Production reranking with graceful fallback to initial order
example.code:
def rerank_with_fallback( query: str, results: list[dict], top_k: int = 5, timeout: float = 2.0 ) -> list[dict]: """Rerank with graceful fallback to initial order.""" import signal
def _handler(signum, frame): raise TimeoutError
try: signal.signal(signal.SIGALRM, _handler) signal.alarm(int(timeout)) from sentence_transformers import CrossEncoder model = CrossEncoder("cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-12-v2") pairs = [[query, r["content"]] for r in results] scores = model.predict(pairs) for r, s in zip(results, scores): r["rerank_score"] = float(s) signal.alarm(0) return sorted(results, key=lambda x: x["rerank_score"], reverse=True)[:top_k] except Exception: return results[:top_k] # fallback: initial retrieval order
example.note: signal.SIGALRM is Unix-only. On Windows, use threading.Timer or asyncio.wait_for for the timeout mechanism.

rule.statement: Every production reranking call MUST have a fallback path that returns initial retrieval results when the reranker fails, times out, or is unavailable. Never let a reranker failure propagate as a query failure.
rule.rationale: The reranker is an accuracy improvement, not a correctness requirement. Initial retrieval results are useful; zero results or an exception is not. Failing silently to initial order preserves user experience.

rule.statement: Cache reranked results by hash(query + sorted(doc_ids)). Identical queries with the same candidate pool recur frequently in chat interfaces where the same context window is reused across turns.
rule.rationale: Reranking is computationally expensive (100-500ms). Caching avoids redundant scoring when the same query is issued repeatedly within a session or across concurrent users asking similar questions.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: In an agentic RAG system, a retrieval subagent fetches an initial candidate pool (top 50-100 via vector search), then passes (query, documents[]) to a reranking step. The reranker (cross-encoder or Cohere API) returns scored pairs, and the agent selects the top-k for context assembly. The reranking step is synchronous and blocking — the generation agent must not proceed until reranking is complete.

rule.statement: LLM-based reranking prompts MUST constrain output to JSON with a defined schema. Without schema enforcement the agent may produce freeform ranking prose that breaks the downstream parser.
rule.rationale: LLMs produce variable-format output when unconstrained. A ranking expressed as natural language prose ("the first document seems most relevant...") cannot be parsed into a sorted index. JSON with explicit index and score fields is parseable deterministically.

list.item: sentence-transformers — local cross-encoder reranking. pip install sentence-transformers. https://www.sbert.net
cohere — Cohere Rerank API (rerank-english-v3.0, rerank-multilingual-v3.0). pip install cohere. https://docs.cohere.com/docs/reranking
flashrank — lightweight local reranker (ms-marco, MiniLM variants), designed for CPU. pip install flashrank. https://github.com/PrithivirajDamodaran/FlashRank
ragatouille — ColBERT late-interaction reranking. pip install ragatouille. https://github.com/bclavie/RAGatouille
llama-index-postprocessor-cohere-rerank — LlamaIndex Cohere rerank postprocessor. pip install llama-index-postprocessor-cohere-rerank
Cohere Rerank (SaaS) — REST/Python SDK. Best managed option; rerank-multilingual-v3.0 covers 100+ languages.
Jina AI Reranker (SaaS) — REST. jina-reranker-v2-base-multilingual; free tier available.
Mixedbread AI (SaaS) — REST/Python. mxbai-rerank-large-v1; strong BEIR benchmark scores.
Voyage AI (SaaS) — REST. voyage-rerank-2; especially strong for code and technical content.
vLLM (OSS) — OpenAI-compatible API. Run cross-encoders locally as a service; GPU recommended.

rule.statement: For multilingual content, use Cohere rerank-multilingual-v3.0 or Jina jina-reranker-v2-base-multilingual. Never use an English-only model (ms-marco family) on non-English content.
rule.rationale: English-only models silently produce degraded scores on non-English text. The multilingual variants cover 100+ languages and maintain precision across language boundaries.

rule.statement: On CPU-only infrastructure, use FlashRank instead of sentence-transformers CrossEncoder. FlashRank is designed for CPU inference and delivers 3-5x faster scoring without requiring GPU.
rule.rationale: sentence-transformers CrossEncoder defaults to CPU but is not optimized for it. FlashRank uses quantized models and CPU-optimized inference paths, making local reranking viable on commodity servers.
